name: Proto-Gulf Arabic

allophone_rules.notes: Velar affrication (kashkasha): /k/ → [tʃ] adjacent to a high front vowel /i, iː/. Alshammari 2026 (JLTR 17(4):1333-1341), literature review p.1335: 'Mustafawi (2006, 2007) examines the affrication phenomenon of velar stops /k/ and /g/ in Qatari Arabic (QA)... She proposes that the velar stop undergoes affrication only in the stem when adjacent to the high front vowels /i/ or /i:/. Conversely, affrication is blocked by the presence of any other segments including front vowels that are [-high].' The general Gulf/Peninsular pattern (attributed there to Ingham 1994; Holes 1991) affricates /k/ to [tʃ] 'in the presence of front vowels'. LIMITATIONS: the alternation is historically lexicalised — per Johnstone (1967), as reported in Alshammari 2026 p.1335, velar affrication 'is clearly no longer productive in Eastern Arabic' and /tʃ/ has 'attained the status of an independent phoneme'. It is further blocked by adjacent emphatics and by the OCP near /s z/. This rule models only the regular onset case (following high front vowel); coda affrication (e.g. samak → simatʃ) and emphatic/OCP blocking are not modelled. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic, so the conditioning vowel is a written kasra/yāʔ.

Velar affrication of /ɡ/ (< Old Arabic *q, realised [ɡ] in Bedouin/sedentary Gulf) → [dʒ] adjacent to a high front vowel /i, iː/. Alshammari 2026 abstract (p.1333) and p.1335: 'the affrication of /g/ to [dz] coincides with what has been reported in other Arabic dialects, where the conditioning environment is strictly limited to high front vowels', and /ɡ/ 'requires a higher degree of vocalic frontness and height to undergo affrication, compared to its voiceless counterpart /k/'. Matar (1969), as reported there, finds Kuwaiti /ɡ/ → [dʒ] adjacent to front vowels, including the emphatic context ðˤiːg → ðˤiːdʒ 'tightness'. LIMITATIONS: Matar's non-adjacent trigger (a front vowel across an intervening /r, l/, e.g. qariːb → dʒiriːb) and emphatic/OCP effects are not modelled — only the adjacent following high-front-vowel case.

ancestors.notes: Peninsular Arabic base — interdental retention and emphatic (pharyngealisation) spreading inherited via the AR_PEN_EMPH_* allophone rules.

notes: Proto-Gulf Arabic (Khaleeji) — abstract ancestor node for Emirati (ar-AE), Bahraini (ar-BH), Kuwaiti (ar-KW) and Qatari (ar-QA); Omani (ar-OM) is genetically Peninsular and inherits ar-x-peninsular directly. DEFINING FEATURES modelled here and inherited by descendants via graphemes_base/OVERLAY_BY_ID: (1) Old Arabic *q → [ɡ] (Bedouin/sedentary Gulf), with [q] retained as a secondary MSA/formal candidate; (2) velar affrication /k/ → [tʃ] and /ɡ/(<*q) → [dʒ] adjacent to a high front vowel /i, iː/ (allophone_rules GULF_K_AFFRICATION, GULF_G_AFFRICATION); (3) interdentals /θ ð ðˤ/ RETAINED, unlike sedentary Levantine/Egyptian which merge them to stops/sibilants — a Bedouin-type retention (grapheme mappings for ث ذ ظ list the interdental first); (4) jiim /dʒ/ ~ [j] variation (glide realisation common in the eastern Gulf); (5) integrated /p/, /v/ from loanwords (پ, ڤ). Emphatic spreading (low vowels back to [ɑ]/[ɑː] next to /tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ zˤ/) is inherited from ar-x-peninsular (AR_PEN_EMPH_*). Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic. Grounded in sources actually read: Alshammari 2026 (velar affrication, whose literature review surveys Mustafawi's Qatari and Matar's Kuwaiti data), Al-Balushi 2016 (Omani/Eastern-Arabian reflexes), Szreder & Derrick 2023 (Emirati affricate variability).

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Northern Najdi study whose literature review (p.1335) surveys Gulf velar affrication: Mustafawi (2006, 2007) Qatari — /k/,/ɡ/ affricate only adjacent to high front /i, iː/, blocked by [-high] and emphatics/OCP; Matar (1969) Kuwaiti /ɡ/→[dʒ]; Johnstone (1967) affrication lexicalised in Eastern Arabic; Ingham (1994), Holes (1991) general Gulf.

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections (pp. 85-90, open-access PDF). Documents Eastern Arabian /q/ reflexes (Bedouin [ɡ]; sedentary [q]; northern mountains [k]; Sur/Sohar [dʒ]), /k/→[tʃ] affrication Bedouin 'in the vicinity of front vowels' vs unconditional in sedentary (Holes 2013), jiim /dʒ/~[ɡ] variation, and interdental/emphatic retention.

sources.author: Szreder, M. & Derrick, D.
sources.title: Phonological conditioning of affricate variability in Emirati Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association
sources.notes: Abstract and published summary read (full text paywalled — cited only for what was read). Finds the [k~tʃ] alternation is a completed phonemic change (lexicalised) while [dʒ~j] is a synchronic process; a nearby similar coronal obstruent inhibits the (voiced) [j] variant of the dʒ~j alternation, whereas /k/-affrication to [tʃ] is not inhibited by similar consonants.
